title: Cat Vomiting Clear Liquid: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your cat is vomiting clear liquid, determine if it is an emergency, and understand why a photo can help your vet with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Clear liquid vomit in cats typically consists of gastric juices and water; while a single episode may be minor, frequent vomiting or lethargy requires urgent veterinary attention to prevent rapid dehydration.

What does it mean when my cat vomits clear liquid?

When a cat vomits clear liquid, it is often a combination of gastric juices, saliva, and any water they have recently consumed. Unlike vomiting undigested food or thick yellow bile, clear liquid usually indicates that the cat's stomach is empty but something is causing irritation or an inability to keep fluids down.

Is clear liquid vomit a veterinary emergency for my cat?

  • Urgency Level: Medium.
  • A single instance may be a minor stomach upset, but it should still be monitored closely.
  • It becomes a high-priority situation if the vomiting persists more than twice in 24 hours.
  • Immediate care is needed if your cat is lethargic or unable to keep water down, as cats can dehydrate very quickly.

How does taking a photo of my cat's vomit help with triage?

  • Provides your veterinarian with critical diagnostic clues regarding volume and consistency.
  • Helps identify hidden flecks of blood or foreign material that may not be obvious to the naked eye.
  • Allows a professional to more accurately determine if the issue is simple gastritis or a potentially life-threatening intestinal blockage.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, vomiting clear liquid can stem from dietary indiscretion or intolerance, including ingestion of novel, spoiled, or contaminated foods or foreign material. Other causes include drug or toxin ingestion (e.g., antibiotics, NSAIDs, corticosteroids, plants, chemicals), systemic illnesses such as pancreatitis, uremic gastropathy or hypoadrenocorticism, endoparasitism (e.g., Ollulanus sp), or bacterial (e.g., Helicobacter-associated disease) or viral infections (e.g., feline panleukopenia). Initial management involves withholding food for a short period and offering small amounts of water frequently, followed by an easily digested diet. Careful monitoring for persistent vomiting, depression, abdominal discomfort, and/or fever is crucial; reevaluation is warranted if vomiting persists. Diagnostic efforts should include abdominal palpation to check for foreign bodies or discomfort, oral cavity examination for linear foreign bodies, and abdominal radiographs to assess for radiopaque objects or intestinal distention indicative of obstruction.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 390)

faqs.question: Could my cat be vomiting from eating too fast?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats that gulp water or eat too quickly may regurgitate clear fluid or undigested food almost immediately after consumption.

faqs.question: When should I call the vet immediately?
faqs.answer: You should seek veterinary care immediately if the clear vomit is accompanied by hiding, a painful abdomen, or if the cat is also unable to pass a stool.

faqs.question: Is dehydration a risk with clear vomiting?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Because clear liquid often consists of water and electrolytes, repeated episodes can lead to rapid dehydration and secondary health complications.
